county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Keith County formed 1873 from Lincoln County. County seat is Ogallala. Western Nebraska. Famous cowtown and Texas Trail end point. Lake McConaughy. Union Pacific Railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: Keith County (formed 1873, seat Ogallala) in western Nebraska was carved from Lincoln County. Records are at the courthouse in Ogallala. Ogallala was a famous Union Pacific Railroad cowtown and the end of the Texas Trail cattle drives in the 1870s-1880s. Lake McConaughy (Kingsley Dam, 1941) is a major feature. Homestead and Timber Culture Act entries at BLM GLO document settlers. Cattle ranching and irrigated farming are the economic base. Anglo-American and German settlers predominated. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Keith County's Boot Hill Cemetery reflects its cowtown heritage. The Front Street museum in Ogallala preserves local history.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Keith County. County Register of Deeds in Ogallala holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Keith County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Keith County Register of Deeds, Ogallala
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1873 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Keith County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1873. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Keith County Probate and Estate Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1873. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.source_name: Keith County Federal and State Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Keith County (1880-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1890 federal census destroyed nationally; Nebraska 1885 state census survives

source_profiles.source_name: Keith County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: History Nebraska / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Keith County communities. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage announcements, homestead proof notices, estate settlements, and community news. History Nebraska holds extensive microfilm of Nebraska newspapers. Chronicling America has digitized selections.

source_profiles.source_name: Kinkaid Act Homestead Records -- Keith County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kinkaid Act (1904) homestead records for Keith County. The Kinkaid Act allowed 640-acre homestead claims in the Sandhills and other western Nebraska counties, recognizing that 160 acres was insufficient for ranching in semi-arid grasslands. Original case files at NARA; patents at BLM GLO.

source_profiles.source_name: Union Pacific Railroad Land Records -- Keith County
source_profiles.repository: Union Pacific Historical Collection / History Nebraska / Nebraska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Union Pacific Railroad land grant and sale records for Keith County. The UP received alternate sections of land along its right-of-way. Land department records document sales to settlers, town lot sales, and colony arrangements. UP Historical Collection at History Nebraska.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Keya Paha County formed 1884 from Brown County. County seat is Springview. North-central Nebraska. Name is Lakota for 'turtle hill.' Niobrara River area. Sparsely populated.
county_metadata.research_tips: Keya Paha County (formed 1884, seat Springview) in north-central Nebraska was carved from Brown County. Records are at the courthouse in Springview. The name is Lakota for 'turtle hill.' This sparsely populated county was settled by homesteaders and ranchers in the 1880s. BLM GLO records document land entries. The Niobrara River borders the county to the north. Anglo-American and German settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited coverage. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. Researchers should check neighboring Brown, Boyd, and Rock county records for related families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Keya Paha County. County Register of Deeds in Springview holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kimball County formed 1888 from Cheyenne County. County seat is Kimball. Nebraska's westernmost county. Union Pacific mainline. Borders Wyoming and Colorado.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kimball County (formed 1888, seat Kimball) is Nebraska's westernmost Panhandle county, bordering both Wyoming and Colorado. Records are at the courthouse in Kimball. The Union Pacific Railroad mainline runs through Kimball. Homestead and Timber Culture Act entries at BLM GLO document settlers. Oil exploration in the 1950s-1960s brought economic growth. Kimball was formerly a Minuteman missile base area. German-Russian and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The 1885 state census may cover this area under Cheyenne County (Kimball was carved from Cheyenne). The Kimball County Historical Society may have local materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Kimball County. County Register of Deeds in Kimball holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

History Nebraska holds manuscript collections. Most trail traffic passed through before county organization.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Knox County formed 1857 from L'Eau Qui Court (extinct). County seat is Center. Northeast Nebraska. Santee Sioux Reservation in the northern part. Niobrara River. Missouri River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Knox County (formed 1857, seat Center) in northeast Nebraska was originally part of L'Eau Qui Court County (extinct). Records are at the courthouse in Center. Knox County is notable for the Santee Sioux Reservation in the northern part of the county -- researchers with Santee Dakota connections should check NARA Kansas City for Santee Agency records, tribal census rolls, allotment records, and school records. Niobrara was the original county seat and an important Missouri River town. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document non-Native settlers. German, Czech, and Irish settlers were significant. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska holds related materials. The Santee Normal Training School records document Native American education.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Knox County. County Register of Deeds in Center holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Santee Sioux Agency Records -- Knox County
source_profiles.repository: NARA Kansas City / Bureau of Indian Affairs
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA agency records for the Santee Sioux Reservation in northern Knox County. Tribal census rolls, allotment records, school records (Santee Normal Training School), and agency correspondence. The Santee Dakota were relocated from Minnesota after the 1862 conflict.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lancaster County formed 1855 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Lincoln (state capital since 1867). Home of History Nebraska (Nebraska State Historical Society). University of Nebraska-Lincoln.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lancaster County (formed 1855, seat Lincoln) contains Nebraska's state capital. Records are at the Lancaster County Courthouse in Lincoln. Lincoln is the seat of state government and home to History Nebraska (Nebraska State Historical Society), the central repository for state records and the single most important genealogical resource in the state. The University of Nebraska-Lincoln's Archives and Special Collections hold manuscript collections, Nebraska authors, and regional history. The Lincoln City Libraries have city directories from the 1880s and local history collections. Lancaster County Register of Deeds has online access to land records. Homestead entries at BLM GLO, territorial-era records, and state census records (1885) are all available. German, Czech, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Lancaster County records. The Nebraska State Genealogical Society (formerly in Lincoln) provides statewide research support. Lincoln newspapers from the 1860s at History Nebraska are valuable sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Lancaster County Register of Deeds online access to land records. BLM GLO patents at glorecords.blm.gov for original federal entries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lincoln County formed 1860 from unorganized territory. County seat is North Platte. Large western county. Union Pacific division point. Buffalo Bill Cody home. Oregon/California/Mormon Trail corridor.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County (formed 1860, seat North Platte) is one of Nebraska's larger western counties spanning the Platte Valley. Records are at the courthouse in North Platte. North Platte was a Union Pacific Railroad division point and home of Buffalo Bill Cody -- the Buffalo Bill Ranch State Historical Park preserves this heritage. Fort McPherson National Cemetery is in Lincoln County. The Lincoln County Historical Museum has local collections. Homestead and Timber Culture Act entries at BLM GLO are key land records. The Oregon, California, and Mormon Trails all passed through this area. Anglo-American, German, and Scandinavian settlers predominated. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. North Platte's early newspapers from the 1860s-1870s are valuable sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Lincoln County. County Register of Deeds in North Platte holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Logan County formed 1885 from Custer County. County seat is Stapleton. Sandhills-edge county. Small population. South Loup River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Logan County (formed 1885, seat Stapleton) is a small Sandhills-edge county carved from Custer County. Records are at the courthouse in Stapleton. Settlement came through homesteading in the 1880s. BLM GLO records document land entries. The South Loup River runs through the county. Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited coverage. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. Researchers should check parent Custer County for earlier records. The small population means limited local genealogical infrastructure.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Logan County. County Register of Deeds in Stapleton holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Kinkaid Act Homestead Records -- Logan County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kinkaid Act (1904) homestead records for Logan County. The Kinkaid Act allowed 640-acre homestead claims in the Sandhills and other western Nebraska counties, recognizing that 160 acres was insufficient for ranching in semi-arid grasslands. Original case files at NARA; patents at BLM GLO.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Loup County formed 1883 from unorganized territory. County seat is Taylor. Central Sandhills. One of Nebraska's smallest counties. North Loup River. Cattle ranching.
county_metadata.research_tips: Loup County (formed 1883, seat Taylor) in the central Sandhills was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Taylor. This is one of Nebraska's smallest counties by population. The North Loup River runs through the county. Cattle ranching is the primary industry. Homestead and Kinkaid Act entries at BLM GLO document settlers. Anglo-American and German settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited coverage. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. Researchers should check neighboring Garfield, Blaine, and Custer county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Loup County. County Register of Deeds in Taylor holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Loup County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Loup County Register of Deeds, Taylor
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1883 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Loup County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1883. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Loup County Probate and Estate Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1883. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.source_name: Loup County Federal and State Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Loup County (1900-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1890 federal census destroyed nationally; Nebraska 1885 state census survives

source_profiles.source_name: Loup County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Loup County including homestead entries, pre-emption claims, timber culture entries, cash sales, and military warrant locations. Nebraska is a public land state -- all original land transfers from federal ownership are documented here. Searchable online by name and location.

source_profiles.source_name: Loup County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Historical Society
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Loup County Historical Society / Nebraska State Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions and burial records for Loup County. Find A Grave has growing coverage. Township, church, and family cemeteries are common in rural Nebraska. County historical societies and the Nebraska State Genealogical Society have published cemetery transcription volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Loup County Records on Microfilm -- History Nebraska
source_profiles.coverage_notes: History Nebraska (NSHS) holds microfilm copies of Loup County records including deed indexes, probate records, marriage records, county board proceedings, and selected court records. Also holds manuscript collections, photographs, and maps related to Loup County history.

source_profiles.source_name: Kinkaid Act Homestead Records -- Loup County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kinkaid Act (1904) homestead records for Loup County. The Kinkaid Act allowed 640-acre homestead claims in the Sandhills and other western Nebraska counties, recognizing that 160 acres was insufficient for ranching in semi-arid grasslands. Original case files at NARA; patents at BLM GLO.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Madison County formed 1856 from unorganized territory. County seat is Madison. Norfolk is the largest city and northeast Nebraska commercial center. Strong German heritage. Elkhorn Valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Madison County (formed 1856, seat Madison) records are at the courthouse in Madison. Norfolk (in Madison County) is the largest city and commercial center of northeast Nebraska. The Elkhorn Valley Museum in Norfolk has regional genealogical collections. Madison County attracted strong German settlement -- German Lutheran and Catholic church records are important. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document original settlers. Norfolk was a Union Pacific branch line town. The Madison County Historical Society preserves local materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Norfolk newspapers from the 1870s are valuable.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Madison County. County Register of Deeds in Madison holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Madison County German Lutheran and Catholic Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Omaha / Diocese of Lincoln / Diocese of Grand Island / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German Lutheran and Catholic church records for Madison County congregations. Baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials. Records often in German. German-Russian (Volga German) congregations may have separate records.

source_profiles.source_name: Madison County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: History Nebraska / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Madison County communities. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage announcements, homestead proof notices, estate settlements, and community news. History Nebraska holds extensive microfilm of Nebraska newspapers. Chronicling America has digitized selections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: McPherson County formed 1887 from unorganized territory. County seat is Tryon. Sandhills county. One of Nebraska's least populated. Kinkaid Act homesteads.
county_metadata.research_tips: McPherson County (formed 1887, seat Tryon) is one of Nebraska's least-populated counties in the Sandhills. Records are at the courthouse in Tryon. The Kinkaid Act (1904) was essential for settlement, allowing 640-acre homesteads. BLM GLO records document land entries. Cattle ranching dominates the economy. The county has never had more than about 700 residents. FamilySearch has minimal coverage. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Federal censuses from 1900 onward are the primary population records. Researchers should check neighboring Logan, Lincoln, and Keith county records for related families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in McPherson County. County Register of Deeds in Tryon holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.
